title: Lecture 9 Active Management
body:
Lecture 10: Active Management of
Equity and Debt Portfolios, and ETFs
 Course Outline




● Mutual funds (called Managed Funds in Australia)

    – Underperformance and flows

● ETFs

● Closed-end funds (Investment Companies (LICs) or Listed Investment Trusts
 (LITs) in Australia)




                                  FINS 3640                                       2
                                                                              2
 The various types of investment vehicles

● Managed Investment Scheme (MIS) structure or investment trusts are collective
 investment vehicles regulated by the Australian Securities and Investments
 Commission (ASIC) under the Corporations Act 2001.

● Mutual funds & Managed Funds: Investors can redeem units at the net asset value
 (NAV), providing liquidity similar to traditional mutual funds in other countries.

● Closed-end funds: these are known as Listed Investment Companies (LICs) and
 Listed Investment Trusts (LITs). They issue a fixed number of shares or units
 that trade on the ASX, functioning like stocks.

● Exchange-traded funds (ETFs): ETFs are a hybrid investment vehicle, legally
 classified as managed investment schemes. They trade on the ASX like LICs and
 LITs, offering real-time pricing and liquidity throughout the trading day.

● As at the end of 2012, registered investment companies managed 23% of
 households’ financial assets. These companies held 28% of US corporate equities.

                         FINS 3640                                 4
  Core Principles

Transparency

● The Product Disclosure Statement (PDS) provides details on investment
 objectives, strategies, risks, fees, and how to buy, redeem, or switch units. This
 information is standardized to allow for easy comparison across funds.

● Funds file documents with ASIC, which are publicly available.




Reports & Auditing

● Funds must provide audited financial reports at least annually.




Holdings & Voting Disclosure

● Funds disclose portfolio holdings periodically and report on voting actions
 regarding corporate governance issues.

                                     FINS 3640                                        5
     Core Principles
Liquidity

● ETFs and Listed Investment Companies (LICs) offer intra-day, real-time liquidity on the ASX.
    Managed funds provide daily redemption at net asset value (NAV), though liquidity may depend on
    the fund’s structure.

Oversight

● Boards of Responsible Entities (RE) for managed funds and ETFs: At least 40% of the board
    members must be independent from fund management.

Required Compliance Programs:

•   Funds must adhere to regulatory requirements under ASIC oversight, with regular compliance
    checks.

Monitoring:

● Funds are regulated by ASIC and, in some cases, the Australian Prudential Regulation Authority
    (APRA).

Custody & Transactions:

•   Funds must keep assets in strict custody, separate from the fund manager.

•   Restrictions exist on transactions between the fund, fund insiders, and affiliates to prevent conflicts of
    interest.

                                              FINS 3640                                                      6
   Core Principles


Limits on Leverage
● Australian regulations restrict leverage within managed funds, ETFs, and Listed Investment
Companies (LICs) to ensure investor protection and fund stability.



Regulatory Framework:
• Managed Investment Schemes (MIS) must comply with leverage limits as outlined in their Product
Disclosure Statement (PDS) and fund constitution, which are regulated by ASIC.

• ETFs and LICs must disclose their use of leverage and maintain oversight to align with investor
expectations.



Voluntary Restrictions:
• Many funds adopt additional self-imposed leverage limits to provide an extra layer of risk
management, which is detailed in their governance policies and investor materials.




                                            FINS 3640                                               7
     Core Principles

Limits on Manager Remuneration

● Performance-based fees for fund managers in Australia must align with ASIC regulations and
    investor protection standards.

Symmetrical Fee Structures:

•   Performance fees should generally be structured to be symmetrical around a chosen
    benchmark to ensure fair outcomes for both investors and fund managers.

•   The fund's Product Disclosure Statement (PDS) must clearly disclose any performance-
    based fees and their calculation method.

Restrictions on Fee Types:

•   ASIC’s guidelines discourage the use of non-linear fee structures, such as one-sided “bonus”
    or “option” fees, ensuring that performance fees are linked to consistent, linear contracts.

•   Remuneration structures are designed to avoid excessive risk-taking and align fund manager
    incentives with investor interests.

Disclosure and Transparency:

•   Full disclosure of remuneration structures is required to ensure transparency, allowing
    investors to make informed decisions.


                                             FINS 3640                                             8
     Exemptions

● Advantage of Being an MIS, LIC, or LIT: These structures allow fund managers to
 raise capital directly from the public. By listing on the Australian Securities
 Exchange (ASX), LICs and LITs can issue shares or units to investors, enabling them
 to access a wide pool of capital.



● Hedge funds have no limits on

  – Fees

  – Leverage

  – Not required to report holdings

  – Not required to provide daily (or even quarterly!) liquidity




                                        FINS 3640                                      9
  Caveats

● Regulations set a minimum standard for investment funds in Australia, such
 as Managed Investment Schemes (MIS), ETFs, and Listed Investment
 Companies (LICs). However, these regulations do not guarantee fund
 performance or investor returns.

● While MIS and LICs are legal entities distinct from their management
 companies, board independence can vary, and some boards may align more
 closely with management than with investor interests. Governance ratings by
 independent research firms can highlight potential issues where funds may not
 have acted in the best interest of investors.

● Australian regulations mandate comprehensive disclosure in the Product
 Disclosure Statement (PDS) and ongoing reports. However, the presentation
 of such information can sometimes be complex or opaque, making it difficult
 for investors to fully understand fee structures and investment strategies.



                                   FINS 3640                                     10
 Managed Investment Schemes
and Mutual Funds




            FINS 3640
     Survivorship Bias


● Carhart’s (1997) mutual fund database summary statistics:



                                                                 TNA = total net assets
                                                                 MTurn = turnover
                                                                 Max Load = maximum
                                                                 of front-end, rear-end
                                                                 and deferred sales
                                                                 charges as a percentage
                                                                 of the investment




● How large is the survivorship bias?
 The subsequent literature shows that using only live funds compared to live+dead
 funds overestimates performance by 2%
 The difference between live and dead fund performance is about 4%

                                        FINS 3640                                          12
  Underperformance


● Basic summary of a huge literature: mutual fund managers slightly beat (or
 equal) the market before costs, but on net underperform the market. However,
 there are mutual fund managers that do add value; finding these managers is
 hard because there is little persistence. Investors tend to put more capital into
 funds with high past returns but the outperformance of funds is fleeting.

● Wermers (2000) remains probably the most comprehensive study of mutual
 fund performance to date. In a nutshell:

   – S&P500 return = 15.4%

   – Average gross return = 16.9%, average net return = 14.6%

   – Average gross alpha = 0.79%, average net alpha = -1.16%




                                    FINS 3640                                        13
  Underperformance


● Fama and French (2010) examine all active mutual funds from 1984 to 2006
 and use CAPM, Fama-French, and Fama-French + momentum benchmarks:


   rit - rft = ai + bi (rmt - rft ) + si SMBt + hi HMLt + uiUMDt + eit
                                    4-factor model benchmark


● The aggregate active fund essentially holds the market

  – Before fees, it underperforms the market by 0.18%

  – After fees, underperformance increases to 1.13%




                                   FINS 3640                                 14
 Underperformance

                         β          s   h   u

     Underperformance


● Carhart (1997) tries to explain mutual fund alphas, constructed using a multi-factor
  model. He estimates a cross-sectional regression:

                                ai (t) = a(t)+ b(t)xi (t)+ ei (t)
                                where xi(t) is a fund’s characteristic

(sum of front and back-end loads)

●   Mutual fund alphas show little persistence over time

                                                                 FINS 3640                                      17
     Underperformance


●   Some positive CAPM alphas persist, but no fund group has a positive four-
    factor (MKT, HML, SMB, MOM) alpha

     – Reason: winning funds hold high momentum stocks and small cap stocks

     – After adjusting for the high returns on the momentum strategy and SMB,
       winning funds have negative alphas

●   The average fund alpha is NEGATIVE

     – Interpretation: Funds underperform after costs

     – This doesn’t imply there are no skilled managers, but these are hard to find
       and performance is not, on average, persistent

     – The top 10% perform worse than benchmark




                                     FINS 3640                                        18
     Flows


●   Mutual fund investors chase performance: flows into and out of mutual funds
    are driven by past performance (which is not indicative of future performance)




                                                      Source: Chevalier and Ellison (1998)

                                     FINS 3640                                           19
     Fees


●   Fees in mutual funds have been slowly trending down (includes expenses
    ratios and annualized loads). Source: ICI

                                                 FINS 3640                                                 20
     Underperformance


●   Why do investors pick funds that waste money?

     – Fees are not transparent

     – Fees are complicated

       Obscured by payments in kind, called “soft dollars”

       Loads, operating expenses, and 12b-1 (marketing) fees come from assets
       under management, not directly from investors’ pockets

       Most turnover costs are not counted as expenses. There are high trade
       execution costs, including bid-ask spreads and price impact. There are
       high trading commissions, hidden by soft dollars

     – Governance is poor




                                    FINS 3640                                   21
       Underperformance

●   Investors often look only at
    past performance

     – Fund inflows are very
       sensitive to past one-
       year fund returns. This
       is usually the measure
       picked by marketers to
       sell funds




                                   FINS 3640   22
     Underperformance


●   Investors are misled by survivorship bias

     – Fund companies tend to keep top performers. A common claim “75% of
       our funds beat the market.” They kill poorly performing funds

●   Funds use “incubation” periods to test investment strategies and fund
    managers

     – Incubation period = period before the fund is widely distributed




                                      FINS 3640                             23
     Underperformance


●   Incubation allows funds to benefit from chance

     – Idea: start many funds with different strategies

     – Then, select the best ones (selection bias)

     – Backfill their performance history and market them

●   Fund families can “juice” incubation returns

     – Incubating funds get preferential access to good deals




                                      FINS 3640                 24
  Beyond Mutual Funds and Managed Investment Schemes


If the average active equity mutual fund doesn’t add value, what about

● Bond mutual funds? No, see Blake, Elton and Gruber (1993), Ferson, Henry and
 Kisgen (2006), Cigi and Gibson (2012)

● Active institutional funds management? No, it’s as dismal as mutual funds, see
 Busse, Goyal and Wahal (2010)

● Institutional proprietary trading? (eg GS etc, but heavily curtailed under Dodd-
 Frank 2010) Unknown, but presumably yes.

● Hedge funds? (eg Bruce Kovner, Bill Eckhardt etc) For most, not really.




                                     FINS 3640                                       25
 ETFs




       FINS 3640
      ETFs

● Total net assets of ETFs are $1.34 trillion at year end 2012 (ICI statistics), but are still
  dwarfed by mutual funds ($13.05 trillion)

● The first ETF (S&P500 SPDR) in the US was introduced in 1993 after the fund
  sponsor received SEC exemptive relief to allow “in-kind” creation and for share to
  trade throughout the day at a price different from NAV.*

● ETFs are required to publish portfolio holdings daily.               This is called the “creation
  basket”.

● ETFs can be bought or sold in real-time at intra-day prices



* The Toronto Index Participation ETF listed on the TSE tracking the Toronto 35 is acknowledged to be the first




                                                  FINS 3640                                                       27
  ETFs


ETFs vs Mutual Funds

● ETFs offer immediate liquidity.Mutual funds are “forward priced” daily
 (usually at 4pm EST) and all investors receive the same price.

● ETFs tend to be more tax efficient, and sometimes even more efficient than
 index mutual funds that do little trading. Mutual funds must sell off securities to
 pay off investors redeeming shares, which produces capital gains and a
 resulting tax liability. These are passed through to investors.

● ETF holdings are published daily.   In contrast, mutual fund disclosure of
 holdings is quarterly.

● ETFs can be shorted, mutual funds can only be held long.




                                      FINS 3640                                        28
 Closed-End Funds

(Investment Companies (LICs) or
Listed Investment Trusts (LITs) in
Australia)




               FINS 3640
  Closed-End Funds


● A closed-end fund has its shares listed on a stock exchange (like ETFs), but
 the number of shares is fixed to investors during an IPO. Closed-end funds
 may make subsequent public offerings (SEOs) to raise additional capital.
 Once issued they trade on an exchange.

● At year end 2012, net total assets in closed-end funds in the US were $265
 billion (compared to $13.0 trillion in mutual funds)

● There is a difference between the market value of closed-end fund shares
 trading and the underlying NAV of the fund investments. This can be a
 discount or a premium, but is, on average, a discount. This is called the
 closed-end fund discount puzzle.




                                     FINS 3640                                   30
 31
 Conclusions

•   The average active mutual fund is hazardous to your
    wealth
•   In your personal portfolio, go index and watch fees




                          FINS 3640
